section.title: The Three Annual Feasts
section.sectionRationale: This chapter completes Deuteronomy's calendar of covenant holiness by establishing three annual pilgrimage feasts, each drawing every household back to the one chosen place to remember God's deliverance and celebrate His provision together.
section.thesis: Moses commands three annual pilgrimage feasts, Passover with its unleavened bread of affliction, the Feast of Weeks with its freewill offering, and the Feast of Tabernacles with its complete joy, each drawing every household back to the one place the LORD chooses to remember His deliverance and celebrate His provision.
section.frameNote: Four movements: Passover and the bread of affliction; the Feast of Weeks and its freewill offering; the Feast of Tabernacles and its complete joy; and the closing instruction that no one appear before the LORD empty-handed.

verses.text: Observe the month of Abib and celebrate the {{pesach|Passover}} to the LORD your God, because in the month of Abib the LORD your God brought you out of Egypt by night.
You are to offer to the LORD your God the Passover sacrifice from the herd or flock in the place the LORD will choose as a dwelling for His Name.

You must not eat leavened bread with it; for seven days you are to eat with it unleavened bread, the bread of affliction, because you left the land of Egypt in haste, so that you may remember for the rest of your life the day you left the land of Egypt.
No leaven is to be found in all your land for seven days, and none of the meat you sacrifice in the evening of the first day shall remain until morning.
You are not to sacrifice the Passover animal in any of the towns that the LORD your God is giving you.
You must only offer the Passover sacrifice at the place the LORD your God will choose as a dwelling for His Name. Do this in the evening as the sun sets, at the same time you departed from Egypt.
And you shall roast it and eat it in the place the LORD your God will choose, and in the morning you shall return to your tents.
For six days you must eat unleavened bread, and on the seventh day you shall hold a solemn assembly to the LORD your God, and you must not do any work.

units.label: The Feast of Weeks: a freewill offering

verses.text: You are to count off seven weeks from the time you first put the sickle to the standing grain.
And you shall celebrate the {{chag|Feast}} of Weeks to the LORD your God with a freewill offering that you give in proportion to how the LORD your God has blessed you,

and you shall rejoice before the LORD your God in the place He will choose as a dwelling for His Name, you, your sons and daughters, your menservants and maidservants, and the Levite within your gates, as well as the foreigner, the fatherless, and the widows among you.
Remember that you were slaves in Egypt, and carefully follow these statutes.

units.label: The Feast of Tabernacles: complete joy

verses.text: You are to celebrate the Feast of Tabernacles for seven days after you have gathered the produce of your threshing floor and your winepress.
And you shall {{simchah|rejoice}} in your feast, you, your sons and daughters, your menservants and maidservants, and the Levite, as well as the foreigner, the fatherless, and the widows among you.

For seven days you shall celebrate a feast to the LORD your God in the place He will choose, because the LORD your God will bless you in all your produce and in all the work of your hands, so that your joy will be complete.

units.label: Three times a year: not empty-handed

verses.text: Three times a year all your men are to appear before the LORD your God in the place He will choose: at the Feast of Unleavened Bread, the Feast of Weeks, and the Feast of Tabernacles. No one should appear before the LORD empty-handed.
Everyone must appear with a gift as he is able, according to the blessing the LORD your God has given you.

pesach.gloss: Passover, from a root meaning to pass over or spare. Commemorates the LORD passing over Israel's homes in judgment on Egypt, the founding act of redemption that every subsequent generation was to remember as though they themselves had left Egypt.

chag.gloss: Feast, pilgrimage festival, from a root meaning to encircle or make a pilgrimage. Describes the three annual occasions when all Israel's men journeyed to the central sanctuary, binding the whole nation together in shared worship and memory.

simchah.gloss: Joy, gladness, rejoicing. Commanded repeatedly at these feasts, it describes worship that is not merely dutiful but genuinely joyful, extended deliberately to include servants, foreigners, the fatherless, and widows in the celebration.

basic.context: Moses establishes three annual pilgrimage feasts, Passover, the Feast of Weeks, and the Feast of Tabernacles, each requiring Israel to gather at the one chosen place to remember God's deliverance from Egypt and celebrate His ongoing provision with genuine, shared joy.
basic.hermeneutics: These are specific covenant festivals tied to Israel's agricultural calendar and redemption history. Their patterns of remembrance, celebration, and joyful inclusion of the vulnerable remain instructive, even as the New Testament shows these very feasts fulfilled in the person and work of Christ.
basic.originalLanguages: Pesach, Passover, commemorates the LORD passing over Israel in judgment on Egypt. Each chag, pilgrimage feast, gathered the whole nation at one place, and simchah, joy, is commanded repeatedly, extended deliberately to servants, foreigners, the fatherless, and widows, not reserved for the comfortable alone.
basic.typology: All three feasts anticipate Christ directly: Passover is fulfilled when Christ, our Passover lamb, is sacrificed, 1 Corinthians 5:7; the Feast of Weeks, later called Pentecost, is fulfilled when the Spirit is poured out on the church, Acts 2:1; and the Feast of Tabernacles anticipates Christ tabernacling among His people, John 1:14, and the final, complete ingathering when God dwells fully with humanity, Revelation 21:3.
basic.christ: Paul calls Christ 'our Passover lamb,' sacrificed for us, 1 Corinthians 5:7, directly fulfilling the festival established here. The Spirit is poured out at Pentecost, the very day of the Feast of Weeks, Acts 2:1, and John says the Word 'tabernacled' among us, John 1:14, using the same imagery as the Feast of Tabernacles, anticipating the final tabernacle of God with humanity, Revelation 21:3.
basic.god: God commands not mere obligatory attendance but genuine joy, simchah, at these feasts, and He insists that joy be shared deliberately with servants, foreigners, the fatherless, and widows, showing that true worship in His presence naturally overflows into inclusive celebration, not private comfort.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Corinthians 5:7 calls Christ our Passover lamb, sacrificed for us. Acts 2:1 places the outpouring of the Spirit on the very day of the Feast of Weeks, Pentecost. John 1:14 says the Word tabernacled among us, echoing the Feast of Tabernacles. Revelation 21:3 describes God's dwelling finally and fully with His people.
basic.oneStory: This chapter builds Israel's whole year around remembering deliverance and celebrating provision together, with deliberate joy extended to the vulnerable. Each of these three feasts finds its fulfillment in Christ, our Passover lamb, the giver of the Spirit at Pentecost, and the one who tabernacles among His people until God dwells with them fully forever.

context.literary: This chapter completes the calendar of covenant holiness that began with the centralization of worship in chapter 12, gathering Israel's whole religious year around three pilgrimage feasts before turning to the appointment of judges and officials in the next section.
context.historical: These three feasts, tied respectively to the exodus, the early grain harvest, and the final harvest of the agricultural year, structured the entire rhythm of Israel's national and agricultural life around remembering God's past deliverance and celebrating His ongoing provision.
context.canonical: The New Testament shows all three feasts fulfilled in Christ and the early church: Passover in the crucifixion, 1 Corinthians 5:7, the Feast of Weeks in the outpouring of the Spirit at Pentecost, Acts 2, and the Feast of Tabernacles in Christ's incarnation and the final dwelling of God with His people, John 1:14, Revelation 21:3.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To build Israel's entire calendar around recurring, communal remembrance of God's deliverance from Egypt and joyful celebration of His provision, ensuring every household, including the vulnerable, shared in that worship together.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific festival calendar, dates, offerings, and pilgrimage requirements describe covenant practices particular to Israel's national life under the old covenant. What is prescriptive and abiding is the underlying pattern: deliberate, joyful, communal remembrance of God's deliverance, generous worship in proportion to blessing received, and intentional inclusion of the vulnerable in celebration.

debates.question: Should Christians today observe these festivals, or does their fulfillment in Christ set them aside entirely?

views.label: Fulfilled and therefore not required as continuing obligations
views.case: Colossians 2:16-17 explicitly says festivals, new moons, and Sabbaths were 'a shadow of the things to come,' but the substance belongs to Christ, suggesting these specific festival observances are fulfilled rather than binding on New Testament believers.

views.label: Their patterns remain instructive even if not obligatory
views.case: While not required as legal observances, many believers find continuing value in the rhythms these feasts model, structured remembrance, harvest gratitude, and joyful, inclusive celebration, as spiritually formative practices, distinct from treating the specific festival calendar as binding law.

debates.bearing: Both views agree these feasts find their true substance fulfilled in Christ, and that no believer is bound to observe them as a requirement for righteousness before God, Colossians 2:16-17, Galatians 4:9-11.

study.originalLanguages: Pesach, Passover, remembers the LORD passing over Israel's homes in judgment on Egypt. Each chag, pilgrimage feast, gathers all Israel at one chosen place, and simchah, joy, is commanded repeatedly and extended deliberately to servants, foreigners, the fatherless, and widows, making worship inherently communal and inclusive rather than private or exclusive.

typology.type: Passover, the sacrifice that spares Israel from judgment
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ, 'our Passover lamb,' sacrificed once for all, 1 Corinthians 5:7.

typology.type: The Feast of Weeks, the firstfruits harvest celebration
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled when the Spirit is poured out on the church at Pentecost, the very day of this feast, Acts 2:1.

typology.type: The Feast of Tabernacles, dwelling in temporary shelters in joyful thanksgiving
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates Christ tabernacling among His people, John 1:14, and the final, complete dwelling of God with humanity, Revelation 21:3.

study.christ: Each of these three feasts finds direct fulfillment in Christ and the church: Paul calls Him 'our Passover lamb,' sacrificed for us, 1 Corinthians 5:7; the Spirit is poured out on the very day of the Feast of Weeks, Pentecost, Acts 2:1; and John describes the Word becoming flesh and 'tabernacling' among us, John 1:14, the same image behind the Feast of Tabernacles, anticipating the day when God's dwelling will be with humanity forever, Revelation 21:3.
study.god: God commands not mere obligatory attendance but genuine, complete joy at these feasts, and He insists that joy be shared deliberately with servants, foreigners, the fatherless, and widows, revealing that true worship in His presence naturally overflows into generous, inclusive celebration rather than private comfort or exclusive privilege.

crossReferences.note: Calls Christ our Passover lamb, sacrificed for us, the direct fulfillment of this festival.
Places the outpouring of the Holy Spirit on the very day of the Feast of Weeks, Pentecost.
Describes the Word becoming flesh and tabernacling among us, echoing the Feast of Tabernacles.
Describes God's dwelling finally and fully with His people, the ultimate fulfillment of the tabernacle imagery.

study.oneStory: This chapter builds Israel's entire year around remembering deliverance and celebrating provision together, with deliberate, complete joy extended to the vulnerable. Each of these three feasts finds its fulfillment in Christ, our Passover lamb, the giver of the Spirit at Pentecost, and the one who tabernacles among His people until God dwells with them fully and forever.

quiz.q: Why does Moses instruct Israel to eat unleavened bread for seven days at Passover (v.3)?

options.text: As the bread of affliction, so they remember for the rest of their lives the day they left Egypt in haste.
options.note: Verse 3 gives this exact reason and purpose.

options.text: Because leavened bread was considered unclean at all times.
options.note: Leavened bread is not forbidden generally, only during this specific seven-day festival.

options.text: Because wheat was scarce during this particular season.
options.note: No such agricultural scarcity is given as the reason; the reason is memorial, tied to the haste of the exodus.

options.text: No reason is given.
options.note: Verse 3 gives an explicit, memorable reason.

quiz.q: Where does Moses insist the Passover sacrifice must be offered (vv.5-6)?

options.text: Only at the place the LORD will choose as a dwelling for His Name, not in any of the towns.
options.note: Verses 5-6 state this restriction explicitly.

options.text: In any town the family happens to be living in.
options.note: Verse 5 explicitly rules this out, restricting it to the one chosen place.

options.text: Only in the wilderness where Israel once wandered.
options.note: No such requirement is given; the location is the future chosen place, not the past wilderness.

options.text: No specific location is required.
options.note: Verses 5-6 give an explicit, restricted location.

quiz.q: How is the Feast of Weeks' offering determined (v.10)?

options.text: A freewill offering given in proportion to how the LORD has blessed each person.
options.note: Verse 10 states this exact principle.

options.text: A fixed amount required equally from every household regardless of income.
options.note: The text specifies a proportional offering tied to individual blessing, not a fixed, equal amount.

options.text: An amount determined entirely by the priests without regard to the individual.
options.note: The offering is described as a personal, freewill response, not a priestly assessment.

options.text: No offering is associated with this feast.
options.note: Verse 10 explicitly describes a freewill offering.

quiz.q: Who is included in the joyful celebration of the Feast of Tabernacles (v.14)?

options.text: Sons and daughters, menservants and maidservants, the Levite, the foreigner, the fatherless, and the widow within the gates.
options.note: Verse 14 names this full, inclusive list.

options.text: Only adult men who own land.
options.note: The list explicitly includes servants, women, children, and the vulnerable, far beyond landowning men alone.

options.text: Only the priesthood and their families.
options.note: The celebration is described as including the whole household and community, not restricted to priests.

options.text: No one specific is named as included.
options.note: Verse 14 names a specific, detailed list of participants.

quiz.q: What instruction is given regarding appearing before the LORD at these three feasts (vv.16-17)?

options.text: No one should appear empty-handed, but everyone should bring a gift according to how the LORD has blessed them.
options.note: Verses 16-17 state this instruction directly.

options.text: Attendance is entirely optional for all Israelite men.
options.note: Verse 16 requires all men to appear at these three feasts; it is not framed as optional.

options.text: Only the wealthy were required to bring a gift.
options.note: Verse 17 says everyone brings 'as he is able,' proportional but universal, not restricted to the wealthy alone.

options.text: No instruction about gifts is given.
options.note: Verses 16-17 give explicit instruction about bringing a gift.

questions: Passover's unleavened bread was called 'the bread of affliction,' meant to keep the memory of deliverance vivid and personal, not distant. What practice helps keep God's past deliverance in your own life vivid rather than a fading memory?

The Feast of Weeks' offering was proportional, given 'as the LORD has blessed you,' rather than a fixed, impersonal amount. Does your own giving reflect an honest, proportional response to how God has specifically blessed you?

The Feast of Tabernacles deliberately included servants, foreigners, the fatherless, and widows in its joy, not just the household's own family. Who is currently outside your circle of celebration that God might be calling you to include?
Israel was told never to appear before the LORD empty-handed. What does coming to worship with intentional, prepared offering, rather than empty-handed or thoughtless, look like for you this week?

These three feasts built rhythms of remembering deliverance and celebrating provision directly into Israel's calendar year after year. What regular rhythms in your own life currently draw you back to remembering and celebrating what God has done?

translationNotes.note: 'The bread of affliction' translates lechem oni, bread associated with hardship, deliberately keeping the memory of Egypt's affliction and the haste of departure alive at every Passover meal.

Counting seven weeks from the firstfruits harvest gives the Feast of Weeks its later Greek name, Pentecost, meaning fiftieth, directly connected to Acts 2's account of the Spirit's outpouring on that very festival.

'All your men' reflects the specific pilgrimage requirement for male heads of households and adult men, though verses 11 and 14 make clear that women, children, servants, and the vulnerable were fully included in the feast's celebration itself.
